extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a resistive heat‑bed, an internal fan, two interior temperature sensors and one exterior temperature sensor. The hardware is driven by a Raspberry Pi that runs a bang‑bang‑style controller. Its purpose is to raise the air temperature inside the box to a set point and maintain it despite variations in the content, assuming uniform air temperature generated by the fan. The incubator embodies a small cyber‑physical system with clearly defined cyber components (Raspberry Pi, controller software, RabbitMQ communication) and physical components (heater, fan, sensors, enclosure).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (1) real‑time visualization of incubator status via a time‑series database and dashboard; (2) on‑demand calibration of the thermal models by automatically executing the calibration routine described in section 3.3; (3) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation using a Kalman filter applied to Model B together with live sensor data; (4) what‑if simulation capability through co‑simulation that replays historic or live PT data to evaluate controller parameter changes; (5) a self‑adaptation loop that detects deviations, triggers experiments, re‑calibrates models, updates the Kalman filter and re‑configures the controller autonomously. These services cover monitoring, prediction, optimisation and closed‑loop control for the incubator.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT is built on an open‑source software stack hosted on GitHub. Core components run on a Raspberry Pi and include a low‑level driver that reads temperature sensors and issues actuator commands, and a bang‑bang controller that publishes its state. Inter‑process communication is provided by a RabbitMQ message broker. Sensor readings and controller states are stored in a time‑series database and displayed on a web‑based dashboard (plotting data into a dashboard). Model calibration and tracking are performed by custom calibration algorithms (section 3.3) that update the parameters of Model B. A Kalman filter implementation is used for state estimation and anomaly detection. What‑if simulations are executed via a co‑simulation framework that can ingest historic or live PT data. All code, datasets and deployment scripts are available in the public GitHub repository https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The low‑level driver periodically reads temperature sensors and uploads the measurements to the RabbitMQ communication server; the controller listens to these messages and publishes its control commands. The DT subscribes to the same RabbitMQ topics, stores the incoming stream in a time‑series database and updates visualisations on a dashboard in near real‑time. No explicit sampling period, latency requirement, or temporal granularity is specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The documentation describes a single Digital Twin instance that centrally aggregates data from the Physical Twin via RabbitMQ; no hierarchical, decentralized, or multiple‑instance arrangement is mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a service‑oriented architecture coordinated through the RabbitMQ message broker (AMQP). Core DT services—calibration, Kalman‑filter‑based anomaly detection, what‑if simulation, and the self‑adaptation loop—subscribe to controller and driver messages, exchange data via the broker, persist streams in a time‑series database, and present results on a dashboard. This component integration constitutes the overall orchestration of the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external components relies on the RabbitMQ broker for message exchange (AMQP protocol) between the controller, low‑level driver, and DT services, and on a time‑series database and visualization dashboard for data storage and presentation. No additional enterprise‑level standards or external system dependencies are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator Digital Twin incorporates two analytical thermal simulation models (Model A and Model B) derived from the basic heat‑energy equation Q = c·m·ΔT and the electrical power equation E_power = V·I·Δt. Model A assumes that all supplied electrical power is transferred to the air inside the styrofoam box, while Model B relaxes this assumption to account for the thermal inertia of the heatbed. Both models are parameterised by a small set of free parameters (two for Model A, four for Model B) that are identified through on‑demand calibration routines described in the case study. Real‑time data are obtained from three temperature sensors (two interior, one exterior) that are periodically read by a low‑level driver and published to a RabbitMQ message broker; the data are stored in a time‑series database and visualised on a dashboard. Calibration algorithms consume this sensor stream to update model parameters, while monitoring algorithms (e.g., fault detectors, Kalman‑filter‑based state estimators) use the updated models to generate further knowledge such as anomaly flags or predicted future temperatures.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuators of the incubator are a resistive heatbed (providing on/off heating power) and a fan (providing air circulation). Actuation commands are issued by a Raspberry‑Pi‑based controller that implements a bang‑bang control strategy with configurable lower‑limit (LL) and upper‑limit (UL) temperature thresholds, a heating duration (H) and a waiting duration (C). The controller publishes actuation commands as messages to a RabbitMQ server; the low‑level driver subscribes to these messages, drives the heatbed and fan hardware, and enforces a built‑in safety protocol that prevents unsafe command execution. Actuation is therefore remotely controlled via standard AMQP messages, with the control loop closed by periodic sensor feedback.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensing is provided by three sensors: two placed inside the insulated styrofoam box to measure the internal air temperature and one placed outside to monitor ambient temperature. The low‑level driver periodically reads each sensor (the exact sampling interval is not specified) and transmits the readings to the RabbitMQ communication server, where they are archived in a time‑series database and made available for real‑time visualization and algorithmic processing. No explicit accuracy, precision, or data‑rate specifications are given in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity relies on four explicit assumptions: (1) uniform temperature distribution inside the box (ensured by the fan), (2) negligible heat storage in the box walls, (3) constant specific heat capacity of the internal air, and (4) that the majority of electrical power is converted to heat in the air (relaxed in Model B). Validation is performed by experimental comparison of model predictions against measured temperature traces (section 4 of the study, not detailed here) and by calibrating model parameters on‑line. Uncertainty sources include sensor noise, the simplifying assumptions above, and errors in estimated parameters. On‑demand calibration and a Kalman filter‑based state estimator are used to detect anomalies, re‑estimate the heatbed temperature, and correct model drift, thereby providing a quality‑assurance loop for the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data are acquired by the low‑level driver that periodically reads the three temperature sensors (two inside the incubator and one measuring the room). Each reading, expressed as a numeric temperature value (°C), is packaged into a RabbitMQ message and published to the central RabbitMQ communication server. The message flow is triggered on each sampling instant; the driver applies a built‑in safe protocol but no further preprocessing is described. The Digital Twin can subscribe to these messages to obtain a real‑time time‑series of temperature data.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are generated by the controller (a bang‑bang controller) and sent as RabbitMQ messages to the low‑level driver. Commands include heat‑bed actuation (on/off) and fan control, together with parameters such as lower/upper temperature limits (LL/UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). The low‑level driver validates each command against its safe protocol before driving the physical actuators and can acknowledge execution via a response message.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin uses a RabbitMQ message broker as the technical communication layer. RabbitMQ provides a publish/subscribe topology where the controller, low‑level driver, and DT components publish and subscribe to specific topics. The broker is described as mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy on‑premise or in the cloud, offering reliable message delivery. Specific bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security (e.g., TLS) requirements are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The incubator Digital Twin follows a lifecycle composed of (1) a development phase where the physical hardware (styrofoam box, heat‑bed, fan, temperature sensors) and the software stack (Raspberry Pi controller, low‑level driver, RabbitMQ communication server) are defined (Document 2, 5); (2) a modelling phase in which two simulation models (Model A and Model B) are constructed based on the four assumptions (Document 6); (3) a calibration phase that runs the calibration algorithm (section 3.3) to estimate model parameters from sensor data (Document 1, 4); (4) an operation phase where data from the physical twin are continuously streamed to the DT, monitors evaluate safety and performance, and visualization dashboards display the current status (Document 1, 4); and (5) a self‑adaptation phase that closes the loop by detecting anomalies, planning experiments, re‑calibrating the model, updating the Kalman filter and the controller, and re‑deploying new control parameters (Document 4). Transition between phases is managed through the RabbitMQ communication infrastructure and the calibration/monitoring algorithms, providing governance of the DT throughout its life.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process starts with an open‑source GitHub repository containing hardware and software specifications (Document 2, 5). Software components—the low‑level driver, controller, and RabbitMQ server—are deployed on a Raspberry Pi and exchange AMQP messages (Document 5). Models are created using heat‑transfer equations (Q = cmΔT) under four explicit assumptions (Document 6). Calibration algorithms automatically adjust model parameters from streamed temperature data (Document 1, 4). Real‑time monitoring runs fault detectors and a Kalman‑filter‑based anomaly detector on Model B (Document 4). What‑if simulations are performed via co‑simulation that can ingest historical or live PT data (Document 4). The process is iterative: new experiments trigger re‑calibration, model updates, and controller re‑configuration, constituting the DT evolution (self‑adaptation loop, Document 4). While version control is implied by the public repository, explicit version‑control practices are not described.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides decision‑support insights through (i) real‑time visualization of temperature, fan and heat‑bed states on a dashboard (Document 4); (ii) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation using a Kalman filter that fuses sensor measurements with Model B predictions (Document 4); (iii) what‑if simulation outcomes that forecast temperature evolution under alternative controller settings, enabling optimisation of control parameters (Document 4); and (iv) a self‑adaptation loop that, upon detecting a change (e.g., insertion of a cold object), automatically plans and executes an experiment, re‑calibrates the model, updates the Kalman filter and finally re‑configures the controller, thereby automating the decision‑making cycle (Document 4). Recommendations may be applied automatically or presented to human operators for approval.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. **Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
